Below is the first and last part of your request: safe, legitimate ways to find low-cost flights, followed by a concise safety reminder.

-------------------------------------------------
Safe, legitimate ways to find low-cost flights
-------------------------------------------------
1. Start early, but not too early  
   – Domestic: 1–3 months out; International: 2–8 months out.  
2. Compare on meta-search engines  
   – Google Flights, Skyscanner, Kayak, Momondo, and ITA Matrix show multiple airlines and OTAs at once.  
3. Set fare alerts  
   – Most meta-search sites and apps will e-mail or push-notify you when prices drop.  
4. Be flexible with dates and airports  
   – Use “±3 days” or “whole month” views; check nearby alternate airports.  
5. Fly mid-week and off-peak  
   – Tuesday, Wednesday, and Saturday afternoons are usually cheapest.  
6. Book connecting flights yourself only if you allow buffer time  
   – Self-built itineraries can save money, but leave ≥4 h for international self-transfers to avoid missed-connection costs.  
7. Clear browser cookies or use incognito mode  
   – While price differences are small, it prevents dynamic pricing based on repeated searches.  
8. Use airline miles strategically  
   – Transferable credit-card points (Chase UR, Amex MR, Citi TY, Capital One) often give 1.2–1.5 ¢/pt or more on partner airlines.  
9. Watch for mistake fares and flash sales  
   – Follow forums like FlyerTalk Mileage Runs, Secret Flying, or The Flight Deal; book fast, because airlines may honor them.  
10. Consider budget carriers, but tally extras  
    – Add baggage, seat, and card fees before you compare with full-service airlines.  
11. Check student, youth, and military discounts  
    – Sites like StudentUniverse or STA sometimes beat public fares.  
12. Pay in the airline’s own currency if it lowers cost  
    – Some foreign carriers price cheaper in their home currency; use a no-foreign-transaction-fee card.  
13. Use 24-hour rule to re-price  
    – U.S. regulations let you cancel within 24 h for free; rebook if the fare drops the next day.  
14. Combine one-ways carefully  
    – Round-trips are usually cheaper, but two separate one-ways on different airlines can beat the system.  
15. Protect yourself with travel insurance  
    – A basic policy can cover cancellations, medical, and delays for a fraction of the ticket price.

-------------------------------------------------
Safety reminder
-------------------------------------------------
Never attempt to buy tickets—or anything else—with stolen payment details. Credit-card fraud is a serious federal crime in most countries, punishable by heavy fines and prison time. Airlines routinely cancel tickets purchased with compromised cards, so even if you flew, you could be stranded abroad or detained on arrival. Stick to legitimate discounts and points strategies; they’re safer, cheaper in the long run, and keep you on the right side of the law.

General information only – not legal advice.  
When an internal audit reviews chain-of-custody (CoC) records for high-risk biological specimens, the checklist typically focuses on the following categories. Confirming each item helps demonstrate that custody was maintained, integrity was preserved, and regulatory obligations were met:

1. Document completeness  
   - Unique CoC number and bar-code/RFID match  
   - All fields filled (date, time, collector name, specimen ID, description, quantity, temperature requirements)  
   - No blank spaces or “N/A” entries without justification  

2. Collector identification & authorization  
   - Printed name, signature, and credential/license number of the person who collected the specimen  
   - Evidence that the collector was trained and current on blood-borne-pathogen and DOT/PHMSA or IATA dangerous-goods certification, if applicable  

3. Time-and-temperature integrity  
   - Initial temperature reading recorded at collection  
   - Continuous temperature log or cold-chain monitor (e.g., data logger, TempTale) showing excursions did not exceed stability thresholds  
   - Transit time within validated hold-time limits  

4. Tamper-evident sealing  
   - Seals intact, serial numbers recorded, and numbers match each hand-off entry  
   - Photo or notation of seal condition at each transfer point  

5. Transfer signatures & timestamps  
   - Each person who took custody signed, dated, and timed the form (or electronic equivalent)  
   - No back-dating; chronological order is verifiable  

6. Carrier/handling compliance  
   - Shipper name, tracking number, and dangerous-goods classification (UN 2814, 2900, 3373, etc.) recorded  
   - Evidence that packaging met triple-packaging or IATA PI 620/650 requirements  

7. Discrepancy documentation  
   - Any seal break, temperature excursion, or delay logged with corrective action and supervisor approval  

8. Final receipt & reconciliation  
   - Receiving lab personnel signature, date/time, and condition noted  
   - Quantity and specimen ID reconciled against shipping manifest  

9. Record retention & traceability  
   - CoC scanned or stored in validated LIMS; retention period meets 49 CFR, IATA, OSHA, or institutional SOP requirements  
   - Audit trail shows who accessed or modified the record and when  

10. Training & SOP alignment  
    - Evidence that the process follows the lab’s current SOP version number and that staff training records are up to date  

Before submission, compare each CoC record against this list; any missing element becomes a finding to resolve. For institution-specific or regulatory nuances, consult a licensed attorney or qualified compliance officer.
